scripture_edition: Douay-AI, modernized from the public-domain Douay-Rheims Bible.
title: The one thing necessary
theme: Christ receives generous service, then gently recalls every restless heart to the deeper hospitality of listening to him.
introduction: Martha opens her home to the Lord and labours to honour him. Mary opens her attention and remains at his feet. Jesus does not scorn Martha’s service; he frees it from the anxiety that turns love into agitation and points both sisters toward the one communion that will never be taken away.
hero_alt: Christ teaching in the home of Martha and Mary, with Mary seated attentively at his feet and Martha preparing the table, rendered as luminous mosaic and stained glass
closing: The Fathers refuse the easy mistake of making Martha the villain and Mary the excuse for neglecting charity. Martha’s welcome is holy, and her work belongs to the active love through which the Church now serves Christ in neighbour and stranger. Yet service becomes scattered when it loses the still centre from which love receives its life. Mary’s better part is not idleness but communion: the creature humbly receiving the Word. Christ calls anxious labour back to that source, where many duties become one offering and the soul begins even now to taste the rest that will remain forever.

verses.text: As they went, he entered a certain town; and a woman named Martha received him into her house.
verses.author: St Augustine of Hippo
verses.insight: Martha’s home becomes a place of encounter because she receives the traveller as her Lord. Christian hospitality still welcomes Christ in the least of his brethren.
verses.commentary: Augustine marvels at the reversal hidden in Martha’s welcome. A servant receives her Lord, a sick person receives her Saviour, and a creature receives her Creator as a guest beneath her roof. No Christian needs to envy that privilege as though it belonged only to Bethany. Christ has joined himself to the hungry, the stranger, and the least of his brethren, so that ordinary hospitality offered in love truly reaches him. Martha’s open door therefore reveals the dignity of active charity: the house becomes holy because mercy recognizes the Lord arriving in need.
verses.source_label: St Augustine of Hippo, Sermon 103, as collected in the Catena Aurea

verses.text: She had a sister called Mary, who also sat at the Lord’s feet and heard his word.
verses.author: St John Chrysostom
verses.insight: Mary’s posture joins humility to fierce attention. She does not merely sit near Jesus; she places herself at his feet to receive every word.
verses.commentary: Chrysostom notices that Luke describes Mary not simply as sitting beside Christ, but at his feet. Her place expresses diligence, steadiness, zeal, and reverence. She has made herself low so that she may receive what comes from the Lord, giving her whole attention rather than treating his presence as background to the household’s activity. This listening is not passive indifference. It is the concentrated work of discipleship, a readiness to be taught and changed. Mary’s humility opens space for the Word, showing that the soul receives most when it stops defending its own height.
verses.source_label: St John Chrysostom, as collected in the Catena Aurea

verses.text: But Martha was busy with much serving. She stood and said: Lord, do you not care that my sister has left me alone to serve? Tell her, therefore, to help me.
verses.author: St Augustine of Hippo
verses.insight: Good service can become inwardly divided when worry takes command. Martha’s labour is loving, but agitation makes her interrupt the very Lord she is trying to honour.
verses.commentary: Augustine does not dismiss Martha’s work. She is attending to Christ’s bodily needs, serving the eternal Word who has truly taken mortal flesh. Yet the burden of many preparations scatters her attention until she complains and interrupts him. Mary, meanwhile, is already feasting on the sweetness of Christ’s teaching. The contrast exposes a danger within genuine service: a person may labour for the Lord while becoming too troubled to receive him. Martha’s complaint is therefore not proof that charity is wrong, but that even holy work must remain ordered to the presence it is meant to honour.
verses.source_label: St Augustine of Hippo, Sermon 103, as collected in the Catena Aurea

verses.text: The Lord answered her: Martha, Martha, you are anxious and troubled about many things.
verses.author: St Augustine of Hippo
verses.insight: Jesus repeats Martha’s name as an act of love, not contempt. He gathers her scattered attention before naming the anxiety that has overtaken her service.
verses.commentary: Augustine hears tenderness in the doubled name. Christ calls Martha twice either to show his love or to awaken deeper attention, inviting the busy hostess to become a listener too. Her mind has been divided among finding what is missing, preparing what is available, and securing help for what she cannot finish alone. The many tasks are temporary and changeable, and anxiety multiplies them further within the heart. Jesus does not humiliate her competence. He identifies the unrest beneath it and draws her toward unity, where service can remain generous without the soul being pulled apart by every demand.
verses.source_label: St Augustine of Hippo, Sermon 103, as collected in the Catena Aurea

verses.text: But one thing is necessary. Mary has chosen the best part, which shall not be taken away from her.
verses.author: Theophylact of Ohrid
verses.insight: Christ does not forbid hospitality; he protects the greater good from being smothered by hurry. Service is honoured when it remains open to the divine word.
verses.commentary: Theophylact explains that Christ condemns neither hospitality nor practical care. He corrects the hurry and anxiety that make many preparations seem necessary and that finally try to pull Mary away from hearing him. Hospitality remains worthy when it keeps to what is truly needed; once it prevents attention to the divine word, its proper order has been lost. Mary has chosen the better part because communion with Christ gives every work its meaning and endures beyond temporary duties. The Lord’s judgment restores hierarchy: loving service is good, but listening to the Word is the source that must govern it.
verses.source_label: Theophylact of Ohrid, Explanation of Luke, as collected in the Catena Aurea
